3 min read
Just now

--

Hey there,
This week, attention gets an upgrade, Boris Cherny’s useful theory on five job archetypes in the age of AI, and an AI skill to “map the fog” before you build.
1. Attention Is All You Need: The 2026 Upgrade
2017 Google published a research paper, “Attention is All Your Need.” Its big idea is attention: before an AI predicts the next word, it checks which earlier words are most relevant. This research paper became the foundation for all LLMs . Since ’17, attention has received multiple upgrades.

• 2017 Multi-Head Attention (MHA): A “head” is one way to recognize patterns. For example, one head to track grammar, another might track names & addresses. Multiple heads is crucial since language has many relationships all at once

• Subsequent MQA, GQA & SWA: Techniques to make attention faster & cheaper. MQA has fewer repeated memory lookups, GQA groups attention work together, and SWA looks through a sliding window of nearby text instead checking every word against every other words

• Linear, Sparse & Paged Attention: Linear attention continues to reduce cost as context gets longer, where sparse attention skips less useful token pairs and focuses compute on important ones. Today, paged attention makes the store memory easier to manage at scale
Why it matters: The bottleneck for AI execution has shifted from model capacity to inference efficiency. These deep structural modifications mean models are growing cheaper, faster, and more performant without requiring exponential increases in raw chip compute, making it easier to serve millions of people.
2. Unbundling Job Titles: The Five Archetypes
Boris Cherny, the Anthropic engineer who created Claude Code, recently outlined a framework tracking how AI is fundamentally changing traditional job functions. In highly automated environments, specialized labels like “engineer,” “designer,” or “product manager” explain very little about a person’s actual contribution; instead, teams are reorganizing into five behavior-based archetypes:

• The Prototyper & The Builder: The Prototyper invents ideas. The Builder steps in next, executing the idea and ships the product

• The Sweeper: The Sweeper’s explicit cleans thing up and makes sure the product runs smoothly

• The Grower & The Maintainer: The Grower takes a functioning product and granularly iterates across data points to optimize product, continue to increase product-market fit. The Maintainer keeps the mature system secure, reliable, fast, and efficient
Why it matters: AI probably make stage-of-work more important than the traditional title or position. The question becomes less “what department are you in?” and more “are you good at inventing, shipping, cleaning, growing, or maintaining?”
3. AI Skill of the Day: Map the Fog Before You Build
Complex projects fail fastest when you immediately command an AI agent to “start building”. To protect your workflow, try deploying a planning protocol called Fog Mapping to clarify unknowns before writing a single line of execution instruction.
The Pathfinder Blueprint:
(Drop this structural template directly into your workspace before kicking off an unmapped initiative)
I am starting a large project and want to map the fog before building.
Project Goal: [Describe your desired outcome]
Known Context: [Paste requirements, constraints, stakeholders, links, and relevant notes]
Act like a senior project planner. Create:
1. The decisions that are already fixed
2. The decision frontiers: choices that still shape the project
3. The fog-of-war questions: unknown that could change the plan
4. For each unknown, label the best next move: research, prototype, ask an expert, user test, or delegate
5. A parallel work plan with 3–5 tracks that different people or agents can run at the same time
6. The next three actions I should take today
P.S. We can now detect AI-Written fiction based on plot structure. The biggest difference between AI vs. Human Writing? AI over-explains the themes and much less likely deviate from the narrative arc, where humans are far more comfortable with ambiguous endings and frequently engage outside of the narrative (e.g., “breaking the wall” and engage directly with the reader). Till next week!
Stay in the game,
Zoe
